name: Peña del Hierro y nacimiento del Río Tinto - running circular desde Nerva
name_original: Peña del Hierro y nacimiento del Río Tinto – running circular desde Nerva
region_intro: Jogging routes around Nerva traverse a unique landscape shaped by centuries of mining, featuring the distinctive red waters of the Río Tinto and "Mars-like" terrain. The region's geology, rich in mineral deposits, creates a varied and often challenging environment for running. Extensive open-pit mines and multicolored earth provide a dramatic backdrop for outdoor exercise. These routes often include varied surfaces and gradients, offering an engaging experience for runners. Best jogging routes around Nerva The most popular jogging route is Peña del Hierro y nacimiento del Río Tinto - running circular desde Nerva, a 9.6 miles (15.4 km) trail that takes 1 hour 52 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores the historic Peña del Hierro mine area and the source of the Río Tinto. Another top favourite among local runners is Río Tinto Loop from Berrocal, a difficult 9.5 miles (15.3 km) path. This trail offers an immersive experience through the iconic red landscapes of the Río Tinto. Local runners also love the Cachán Bridge – Riscos Altos Viewpoint loop from Los Frailes, a 20.9 miles (33.7 km) trail leading through varied terrain with significant elevation gain, often completed in about 4 hours 45 minutes. Jogging around Nerva is defined by the surreal red landscapes of the Río Tinto, historic mining areas like Peña del Hierro, and challenging trails with significant elevation changes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult. The routes in Nerva are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 1 review. More than 150 runners have used komoot to explore Nerva's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available around Nerva?
A: There are over 10 dedicated running routes around Nerva, offering a variety of experiences. These range from moderate to difficult, with 5 moderate and 6 difficult options, ensuring there's a trail for different fitness levels.

Q: What kind of unique landscapes can I expect on the jogging trails in Nerva?
A: The jogging trails in Nerva offer truly unique and 'Mars-like' landscapes, primarily shaped by the region's rich mining history. You'll encounter the surreal red waters of the &lt;strong&gt;Río Tinto&lt;/strong&gt;, vast open-pit mines like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5287308" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Corta Atalaya Mine&lt;/a&gt;, and multicolored terrain with shades of red, yellow, and purple. These dramatic backdrops make for an otherworldly running experience.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly running routes in Nerva?
A: The running routes around Nerva are generally considered moderate to difficult due to the varied surfaces and gradients shaped by the mining landscape. While there are no routes explicitly classified as 'easy' in the current selection, runners with some experience can find engaging challenges among the moderate options.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or points of interest I might see while running in Nerva?
A: Many running routes in Nerva pass by significant landmarks. You can explore the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5300427" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña del Hierro&lt;/a&gt; mine area and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4402997" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Río Tinto&lt;/a&gt;. Other points of interest include the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5287308" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Corta Atalaya Mine&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5082202" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Touristic Railway for Rio Tinto Mines&lt;/a&gt;, and the scenic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5345695" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cachán Bridge&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular running routes around Nerva?
A: Yes, Nerva offers several excellent circular running routes. A popular choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e984536943/pena-del-hierro-y-nacimiento-del-rio-tinto-running-circular-desde-nerva" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña del Hierro y nacimiento del Río Tinto - running circular desde Nerva&lt;/a&gt;, a challenging 9.6-mile (15.4 km) loop that takes you through historic mining areas and to the source of the Río Tinto.

Q: What do other runners say about the trails in Nerva?
A: The running trails in Nerva are highly regarded by the komoot community, boasting an average score of 5.0 stars from over 1 review. Runners often praise the unique 'Mars-like' landscapes, the immersive experience along the Río Tinto, and the challenging yet rewarding terrain that helps improve running effectiveness and precision.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the running trails in Nerva?
A: While the guide does not explicitly state whether all trails are dog-friendly, many natural outdoor areas in Spain welcome dogs, especially on leash. Given the rugged and natural environment of Nerva's trails, it's advisable to ensure your dog is accustomed to varied terrain and to carry enough water for both of you. Always check local signage for specific restrictions.

Q: What is the best time of year to go running in Nerva?
A: Nerva, located in Huelva, Spain, experiences hot summers. The best time for running would generally be during the cooler months, from autumn through spring (October to May), to avoid the intense summer heat. Spring offers pleasant temperatures and potentially vibrant landscapes, while autumn provides comfortable conditions for longer runs.

Q: Are there running routes that follow the Río Tinto?
A: Absolutely! The iconic Red River is a major highlight for runners in Nerva. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1014195649/rio-tinto-loop-from-berrocal" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Tinto Loop from Berrocal&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 9.5-mile (15.3 km) path that offers an immersive experience through the river's unique red landscapes. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e984552197/berrocal-rio-tinto-loop-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Berrocal - Río Tinto Loop Trail&lt;/a&gt;, which also explores this distinctive environment.

Q: What kind of terrain and elevation changes can I expect on Nerva's running routes?
A: Nerva's running routes are characterized by varied terrain and significant elevation changes, making them engaging and challenging. You'll encounter surfaces ranging from natural earth to rocky paths, often with steep ascents and descents. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24522911" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cachán Bridge – Riscos Altos Viewpoint loop from Los Frailes&lt;/a&gt; features over 1300 meters of elevation gain, providing a demanding workout amidst the region's dramatic geology.

Q: Is there parking available near the running trailheads in Nerva?
A: While specific parking details for every trailhead are not provided, Nerva is a town with various access points to the surrounding natural areas. For routes starting from towns like Berrocal or Nerva itself, you can typically find public parking options. It's always a good idea to check maps or local information for the most convenient parking spots near your chosen starting point.
tips: This road that runs next to the old Riotinto Mines will give you the impression that you are traveling through another planet. A very peculiar landscape and different from what you may know, there is not too much green in the vicinity but instead you will have a varied palette of colors ranging from ocher, gold, yellow, orange and gray.

This place seems from another planet! It is one of the most important mines (already in disuse) in the Nerva area that were exploited to extract iron ore. Today they can be visited by walking along the authorized trails to maintain safety, since these places are usually dangerous due to possible landslides.

Site also known as Sierra de Padre Caro, marks the source of the Río Tinto which runs through the interior of Huelva for about 100 km until it reaches the Río Odiel.
Its name is due to the reddish color of its waters caused by the weathering of minerals with a high content of heavy metal sulfides that were found and exploited in various deposits along the riverbed.
This wonderful site and the river that is born from here is the subject of scientific studies in collaboration with NASA to analyze a possible analogy with the planet Mars.
